---
title: "The Office (US) — Viewership Report"
author: "Elbek Majidov"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-title: "Contents"
    fig-width: 8
    fig-height: 5
    self-contained: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(dplyr)

# Season-level viewership data (Nielsen / Wikipedia)
office_data <- data.frame(
  Season        = 1:9,
  Year          = c("2005", "2005-06", "2006-07", "2007-08", "2008-09",
                    "2009-10", "2010-11", "2011-12", "2012-13"),
  Avg_Viewers_M = c(5.4, 8.0, 8.8, 8.9, 8.5, 7.0, 7.5, 5.7, 4.4),
  Episodes      = c(6, 22, 25, 19, 28, 26, 26, 24, 25)
)

# Compute season-to-season change
office_data <- office_data %>%
  mutate(Change = Avg_Viewers_M - lag(Avg_Viewers_M))
```

## About the Show

*The Office* is an American mockumentary sitcom that aired on *NBC* from
**March 24, 2005** to **May 16, 2013**. It was created by *Greg Daniels*
as an adaptation of the British series of the same name, originally
created by *Ricky Gervais* and *Stephen Merchant*.

The series follows the everyday lives of employees at the Scranton,
Pennsylvania branch of *Dunder Mifflin*, a fictional paper supply company,
as seen through the lens of a fictional documentary crew. The ensemble cast
is led by *Steve Carell* as the bumbling regional manager *Michael Scott*,
alongside *John Krasinski*, *Jenna Fischer*, and *Rainn Wilson*.

Over its run, *The Office* became one of the most beloved American sitcoms
of the 2000s, winning a **Peabody Award** (2006), multiple **Emmy Awards**,
and two **Screen Actors Guild Awards** for Outstanding Ensemble in a Comedy
Series. It ran for **9 seasons** and **201 episodes** in total.

![*The Office* — NBC Logo](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/The_Office_US_Logo.svg/640px-The_Office_US_Logo.svg.png){width=45% fig-align="center"}

---

## Viewership Statistics

The table below summarises the average U.S. viewership per season in
millions, as measured by **Nielsen Media Research** and reported on Wikipedia.

```{r}
#| label: stats-table

kable(
  office_data %>% select(Season, Year, Avg_Viewers_M, Episodes),
  col.names = c("Season", "Air Year", "Avg. Viewers (M)", "Episodes"),
  align     = "c",
  digits    = 1,
  caption   = "Table 1: The Office (US) — Season-Level Viewership Summary"
)
```

Key headline numbers:

- **Peak season:** Season 4 — `r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[4]`M average viewers
- **Lowest season:** Season 9 — `r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[9]`M average viewers
- **Total episodes:** `r sum(office_data$Episodes)` across `r nrow(office_data)` seasons
- **Biggest single-season gain:** Season 1→2 (+`r round(office_data$Change[2], 1)`M)
- **Biggest single-season drop:** Season 7→8 (`r round(office_data$Change[8], 1)`M)

---

## Viewership Over Time

The chart below shows how average viewership evolved across all nine seasons.

```{r}
#| label: fig-viewership-over-time
#| fig-cap: "Average U.S. viewership per season (millions). Source: Nielsen / Wikipedia."

ggplot(office_data, aes(x = Season, y = Avg_Viewers_M)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2E5EAA", linewidth = 1.3) +
  geom_point(color = "#2E5EAA", size = 4) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(Avg_Viewers_M, "M")),
    vjust = -1.1,
    size  = 3.5,
    color = "#2E5EAA"
  ) +
  # Annotation arrow pointing to the Steve Carell departure drop
  annotate(
    "text", x = 6.3, y = 6.3,
    label    = "Steve Carell\nleaves (S7→S8)",
    size     = 3.2,
    color    = "#E74C3C",
    fontface = "italic"
  ) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 7.0, xend = 7.9, y = 6.2, yend = 5.85),
    arrow    = arrow(length = unit(0.2, "cm")),
    color    = "#E74C3C",
    linewidth = 0.6
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 1:9,
    labels = paste0("S", 1:9)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(3.5, 10.5),
    breaks = seq(4, 10, 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "The Office (US) — Average Viewership per Season",
    subtitle = "Nielsen ratings, millions of U.S. viewers",
    x        = "Season",
    y        = "Avg. Viewers (millions)",
    caption  = "Source: Wikipedia / Nielsen Media Research"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "grey40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )
```

---

## Season-to-Season Change in Viewership

The chart below highlights the gain or loss between each consecutive season.
Green bars indicate a viewership increase; red bars indicate a decline.

```{r}
#| label: fig-season-changes
#| fig-cap: "Season-to-season change in average viewership (millions). Source: Nielsen / Wikipedia."

office_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(Change)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Season, y = Change, fill = Change > 0)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = ifelse(
        Change > 0,
        paste0("+", round(Change, 1), "M"),
        paste0(round(Change, 1), "M")
      ),
      vjust = ifelse(Change > 0, -0.5, 1.5)
    ),
    size     = 3.5,
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#27AE60", "FALSE" = "#E74C3C")) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = 2:9,
    labels = paste0("S", 1:8, "→S", 2:9)
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "The Office (US) — Season-to-Season Viewership Change",
    subtitle = "Green = gain, Red = loss (millions of viewers)",
    x        = "Season Transition",
    y        = "Change in Avg. Viewers (millions)",
    caption  = "Source: Wikipedia / Nielsen Media Research"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "grey40"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1)
  )
```

---

## Analysis of Observed Changes

*The Office* followed a clear arc of rise, peak, and gradual decline over
its nine seasons.

**Rise (Seasons 1–4):**
The show had a rocky start, averaging only
**`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[1]`M** viewers in its abbreviated first
season of just 6 episodes. The breakthrough came in Season 2, when
viewership surged to **`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[2]`M** — a jump of
**+`r round(office_data$Change[2], 1)`M viewers (+`r round(office_data$Change[2] / office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[1] * 100, 0)`%)**,
largely fuelled by *Steve Carell*'s rising movie fame and NBC moving
the show to the coveted Thursday "Must See TV" slot. Viewership continued
climbing through Season 3 and peaked in Season 4 at
**`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[4]`M**.

**Plateau and early decline (Seasons 5–7):**
After the peak, viewership began softening. Season 5 dipped slightly to
**`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[5]`M**, followed by a more pronounced drop
in Season 6 to **`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[6]`M** —
a fall of **`r round(office_data$Change[6], 1)`M** from the prior season.
Season 7 partially recovered to **`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[7]`M**,
boosted by anticipation around *Steve Carell*'s widely publicised departure.

**Sharp decline (Seasons 8–9):**
The exit of *Michael Scott* proved fatal to ratings. Between Season 7
and Season 8, viewership collapsed by
**`r round(office_data$Change[8], 1)`M** — a drop of
**`r round(abs(office_data$Change[8]) / office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[7] * 100, 1)`%**
in a single season, the largest single-season decline in the show's history.
The final season averaged just **`r office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[9]`M** viewers,
only **`r round(office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[9] / office_data$Avg_Viewers_M[4] * 100, 0)`%**
of the Season 4 peak — ending a remarkable but uneven nine-season run.
